This occurred even though I attempted to manually replicate in Python the automated steps performed by `mlr3` and its dependencies. Of course, the writer must acknowledge, here, that the observed poor performance of `scikit-learn` might have resulted from user error, not any deficiencies of the library *per se*. I have not saved the Python results, but I can supply them if requested, since I did retain the original scripts.

[^94]: Again, I did not save the former runtimes directly, but I can reproduce them if necessary with the Python scripts I kept.

Influenced by both software and hardware considerations is another pivotal decision: how to implement parallel processing. As we have seen, a major drawback of DML involves the high time costs that it (and machine learning more generally) oftentimes implies. Mitigating this problem to a considerable degree, fortunately, is parallel processing, a procedure we first encountered in @sec-gen-prep-mi. In brief, parallel processing divides computational tasks across a user-specified number of CPU cores. These are run simultaneously, enabling dramatic runtime reductions relative to tasks completed sequentially on a single core. Owing to `mlr3`'s compatibility with software such as the [`future`](https://future.futureverse.org/) package, parallelization is readily achievable in `DoubleML` for R.[^95] For all fitting tasks, then, I exploit this convenience, writing `future` commands to distribute work across (typically) $N - 1$ cores.[^96]

[^95]: Parallel processing is also possible in `DoubleML` for Python through the [`Joblib`](https://joblib.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) library. For more on parallelization with `mlr3`, see [Applied Machine Learning Using `mlr3` in R](https://mlr3book.mlr-org.com/chapters/chapter10/advanced_technical_aspects_of_mlr3.html#sec-parallelization).

[^96]: I normally parallelize over $N - 1$ rather than $N$ cores to ensure that some memory is available for other non-fitting tasks (e.g., web-browser use), thereby guarding against computer unresponsiveness.

As to hardware, specifically, I run my baseline models on the highest-capacity machine at my immediate disposal: my personal computer, a MacBook Pro (2024) running on the Apple M4 Pro chip, 24 GB of memory, and 12 cores (8 performance and 4 efficiency). These specifications are worth mentioning for a few reasons. First, my machine was able to handle my most demanding baseline-learner tasks generally within the span of 3-4 hours. As such, those wishing to replicate my results on more-powerful systems may be able to do so quicker, but those working with less-powerful ones should anticipate longer runtimes. Second, though relatively powerful as a personal computer, my machine proved unable to complete my models when instructed to utilize more complex learners, as I described in @sec-gen-dml-lrn-dcns. Quality robustness checks are therefore conditional on supercomputing access, which I've acquired and intend to leverage (see @sec-cncl).

##### Hyperparameter Tuning {#sec-gen-dml-fits-tune}

In @sec-gen-dml-lrn-cnds, I explain why confidence in $\hat{\theta}_{0}$---DML's estimate of the true treatment effect, $\theta_{0}$---depends greatly on learner performance. To optimize performance and thus inference, many DML experts argue that an operation common to most machine-learning applications should in fact be "required" of practitioners: hyperparameter tuning (Bach et al, 2024b, p. 2).[^97] Heeding their advice, I subject all my models to the procedure. It is the first step they undergo, and it is the most time-intensive due to the relatively large number of fits quality tuning tends to demand.

[^97]: The import of hyperparameter tuning to quality inference is underlined further in other guides to DML, such as Ahrens et al. (2025, pp. 34-35) and Bach et al. (2024a, p. 32).

To review, most machine learners incorporate one or more "hyperparameters," values users must set but cannot estimate directly from the data.[^98] Rather than arbitrarily setting these values and hoping for high-performance to follow, they may instead proceed more methodically, evaluating automatically-drawn candidates of value combinations in a process called "tuning." Typically, users will define a tuning grid comprised of four essential features: (1.) the hyperparameters to tune; (2.) the resolution, or the number of value combinations to evaluate; (3.) the space, or the ranges from which to sample values; and (4.) the search strategy, or the value-sampling method. They will also specify the number of $K$ folds to randomly split their data. Finally, they will perform "cross-validation." Similar to cross-fitting (see @sec-gen-dml-plr), the procedure first fits learners on training data $I_{k}^{c}$ to obtain estimated nuisance parameter $\hat{\eta}_{0,k}$, with which the learners are subsequently evaluated on test data $I_{k}$. Aggregated instead of the out-of-sample residuals, however, is out-of-sample performance, following a user-specified algorithm and performance metric. This operation is repeated for every combination in the tuning grid, after which the candidate demonstrating the best observed performance is chosen for final fitting. In DML, hyperparameter tuning is also usually repeated for every main and auxiliary equation, meaning the entire process is carried out at least twice for any given model.

[^98]: Succinct definitions of hyperparameters may also be found in [Applied Machine Learning Using mlr3 in R](https://mlr3book.mlr-org.com/chapters/chapter4/hyperparameter_optimization.html) and [Tidy Modeling with R](https://www.tmwr.org/tuning).

My own hyperparameter tuning may be thus generalized as follows:

1.  For each $\mathbf{v}_{i,j,l}$, randomly split the data into $K$ folds.
2.  For each DML equation estimating a part of nuisance parameter $\eta_{0}$ (e.g., $m_{0}$ or $g_{0}$):
    i)  Define a tuning grid with resolution $N$ and a search strategy.
    ii) For each hyperparameter combination $n \in [N]$:
        a.  Fit the candidate on $I_{k}^{c}$ to obtain an estimate of the nuisance part (e.g., $\hat{m}_{0,k}$ or $\hat{g}_{0,k}$).
        b.  With the nuisance-part estimate, evaluate the learner on $I_{k}$.
        c.  Record the performance according to a user-specified metric.
        d.  Repeat for $k \in [K]$.
        e.  Aggregate the performance scores according to a user-specified algorithm.
    iii) Compare the aggregated scores, and select the best-performing candidate.

![DML Tuning Flowchart (Ridge). Note that each "e" represents an evaluation moment, wherein the candidate $\lambda$ fit on $I_{k}^{c}$ is appraised with $I_{k}$.](/Users/briankey/Documents/Academics/Northwestern/dissertation/visualizations/memos/dml_flow_viz_1.png){#fig-dml-flow_1}

To an appreciable extent, I tune hewing to the default settings and recommendations of both `DoubleML` and `mlr3`. The reason I do so is twofold: to preserve model simplicity and to safeguard against selecting the worst-performing specifications.[^99] As indicated above, I cross-validate using folds split from the full sample (i.e., $\mathbf{v}_{i,j,l}$), which is in keeping with the `DoubleML` default.[^100] Though users may optionally cross-validate on each fold drawn for the final DML fits (see @sec-gen-dml-fits-final), I refrain from doing so because it is $K$-times more costly and known to perform similarly to full-sample cross-validation in any case.[^101] For the ridge models in particular, defined by hyperparameters $\lambda$ and $\alpha$, I exclusively tune $\lambda$, since ridge requires $\alpha = 0$.[^102] To do so, I set $\alpha$ properly and specify the space from which the $\lambda$ values will be sampled---namely, the range `mlr3` recommends for models using `glmnet`, the main dependency supporting ridge tasks in the `mlr3` ecosystem.[^103] Furthermore, to improve the likelihood that a representative sample of $\lambda$ values is drawn from the full range, I employ a "grid" search strategy, which simply finds $N$ equally-spaced points between and inclusive of the lower and upper bounds. Though the auto-tuner [`glmnet::cv.glmnet`](https://glmnet.stanford.edu/reference/cv.glmnet.html) effectively has a default resolution of $N = 100$ candidates,[^104] I set $N = 20$ to shorten runtimes, and because increasing $N$ beyond this seemed (in early testing) to neither yield meaningful gains to performance nor substantively change my results. The `glmnet` auto-tuner also has default folds $K = 10$, but I lower this to $K = 5$ to remain consistent with the $K$ I select for the final DML fits (see @sec-gen-dml-plr). Finally, as to the performance metrics, I keep the `mlr3` default of MSE for regression tasks, but I depart from said default for classification tasks, opting for precision-recall area under the curve (PR-AUC) in lieu of classification error. I shall elaborate on this latter choice when I cover Chapter 3 (@sec-ch3), as it is the only instance wherein I consider classification tasks. Irrespective of task, however, I aggregate the performance scores as per the `mlr3` default algorithm, which simply averages the scores found from each $k \in [K]$ evaluation on test set $I_{k}$ per $n \in [N]$ candidate.[^105] In the case of ridge, this results in a mean MSE for each $\lambda$, and that with the lowest mean is chosen for the final DML fits (see @fig-dml-flow_1).[^106]

[^99]: To clarify my logic on the latter point, if default settings are "good enough" for most applications, then they are highly-likely to outperform at least some alternative settings. Hence, the defaults should be regarded as minimally safe, insofar as one can reasonably expect them to not be worst-performing settings.

[^100]: For more on this default---as well as its alternative---see the [`DoubleML` User Guide](https://docs.doubleml.org/stable/guide/learners.html) section on hyperparameter tuning.

[^101]: To be precise, the cost of on-fold tuning is $K_{DML}*K_{CV}$: the number of folds split for DML cross-fitting times the number of desired cross-validation folds. The similar performance of full-sample and on-fold cross-validated tuning is proven in Bach et al. (2024b, pp. 8-9).

[^102]: Recall that $\alpha = 0$ for ridge, $\alpha = 1$ for lasso, and $\alpha$ may be tuned between $0$ and $1$ for elastic net.

[^103]: `glmnet` supports `mlr3` users with not only ridge models, but also lasso and elastic-net models. Importantly, users wishing to tune `lambda` must do so through the $s$ parameter. For an explanation thereof, see `mlr3`'s documentation on the [`glmnet` regression learner](https://mlr3learners.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_learners_regr.glmnet.html). For the recommended tuning space of $s$, see the list of default spaces given by [`mlr3tuningspaces`](https://mlr3tuningspaces.mlr-org.com/reference/mlr_tuning_spaces_default.html).

[^104]: Technically, the default resolution that `cv.glmnet` calls (`nlambda`) is set in `glmnet`. For more on the default, see the [`glmnet` Vignette](https://glmnet.stanford.edu/articles/glmnet.html#commonly-used-function-arguments).

[^105]: See the [Measure Class help file](https://mlr3.mlr-org.com/reference/Measure.html) for more on the default score aggregator.

[^106]: Put differently, with ridge, for each candidate $\lambda$, MSE is computed per $k \in [K]$ evaluation of $I_{k}$. If $K = 5$, there will be five scores, all of which are averaged to form a mean MSE for the candidate. Each $\lambda$ is then compared by this score, and that with the lowest is selected for the final DML fits.

The last major decision I make regarding tuning is to implement grouped resampling. A chief concern of all machine-learning practitioners is "data leakage," wherein identical information finds its way into both the training and test sets. This leads to overly-optimistic assessments of model performance (i.e., overfitting), resulting in degraded predictions on new, previously-unseen data.[^107] Though splitting the data into test sets $I_{k}$ and training sets $I_{k}^{c}$ generally helps combat leakage, since each $I_{k}$ and $I_{k}^{c}$ pair must be disjoint observation-wise (as we saw in @sec-gen-dml-plr), the problem may still rear its head when the data feature natural groupings with unique statistical patterns (e.g., clustering by country, region, and year). For instance, if all $I_{k}$ and $I_{k}^{c}$ contain observations of both countries $i$ and $j$, then the model---whilst evaluating on $I_{k}$---will re-encounter the country-specific patterns it learned training on $I_{k}^{c}$, due to which it will likely generate relatively-accurate predictions and report high performance. However, because the countries appearing in $I_{k}$ and $I_{k}^{c}$ are always identical, the model never considers how it might perform when asked to predict on data for a different country---say $z$. In all likelihood, then, the model is unduly-sanguine about its external validity; and if selected, it will be prone to poor prediction when presented with $z$'s data.

[^107]: Sasse et al. (2025) offer a helpful primer on data leakage.

To tackle this source of leakage, practitioners frequently implement "grouped" resampling, which simply executes set-sorting by group rather than by observation. In this way, training fits are evaluated on how well they predict for not only new observations, but also new groups, since it enforces group-wise mutual exclusivity for every $I_{k}$ and $I_{k}^{c}$ pair. Continuing with the previous example, countries $i$ and $j$ would be assigned to one test set each (e.g., $i \in I_{1}$ and $j \in I_{2}$), such that $i$ and $j$ would also complement each other in the accompanying training sets (e.g., $j \in I_{1}^{c}$ and $i \in I_{2}^{c}$). A model trained on these splits may exhibit "worse" performance initially, since the training fits are asked to predict for a different country with (presumably) different data patterns; but with respect to generalizability, its performance is more realistic, meaning it is likely to give better predictions for country $z$ (and others) if selected.[^108]

[^108]: A useful summary and visualization of grouped resampling can be found in [Applied Machine Learning Using `mlr3` in R](https://mlr3book.mlr-org.com/chapters/chapter3/evaluation_and_benchmarking.html#grouped-resampling).

Although my research agenda is ultimately more interested in inference than it is in predicting on new or future data, *per se*, I still turn to grouped resampling for a few reasons. First, as explained in @sec-gen-dml-lrn-cnds, the quality of inference about $\hat{\theta}_{0}$ in the DML paradigm is only as good as the learners generating it are out-of-sample robust.[^109] Optimizing external validity, then, is essential, hence why adopting grouped resampling---a technique that assists in doing so---is unequivocally sensible. Second, my preprocessing decisions imply the possibility of alternative group specifications, rendering robustness thereto an immediate concern. As we saw in @sec-gen-prep-dw, I determined cases' country codes and region assignments thoughtfully yet discretionally, ineluctably sidestepping other categorical choices. Fortunately, concerns about robustness to these untaken options may be readily allayed through grouped resampling---which, as aforementioned, strengthens robustness to unseen groupings in general---making the procedure all-the-more appropriate.[^110]

[^109]: As a reminder, for finite samples, optimizing out-of-sample performance enhances the reliability of $\hat{\theta}_{0}$ and inference about it, since doing so shrinks $\parallel \hat{\eta}_{0} - \eta_{0} \parallel_{L^{2}}$ and thus the maximum bias of the learner producing $\hat{\theta}_{0}$.

[^110]: Future years and spatial units (i.e., countries and regions) constitute another source of categorical variance, though they prove less problematic: valid inference may still be achieved for the time periods and political geographies defined for each $\mathbf{v}_{i,j,l}$.

Naturally, the primary groupings inhering in panel data $\mathbf{v}_{i,j,l}$ are geography and time---namely, either country or region, as well as year. Simultaneously resampling by the two, however, is virtually impossible---or, at the very least, is sure to prove unjustifiable. For example, because there may only be one match for each country and year (e.g., Germany-2000, Germany-2001, etc.), the total number of country-year groups is equivalent to all $N$ rows. To wit, resampling as such is futile, being no different than not resampling by group in the first place: folds will functionally split by observation if each "group" is, in fact, a single observation. In addition, although cluster-robust DML offers a method of preparing training-test set pairs disjoint along two or more dimensions for the final cross-fits, as we shall see in @sec-gen-dml-fits-final, it is neither available for tuning nor attested in the literature thereof, to the best of my knowledge.[^111] Though doing so admittedly cannot neutralize all sources of data leakage, then, resampling by individual groups seems wiser on balance.[^112] Ultimately, I opt for resampling by spatial units (i.e., country or region), because I intuitively expect my data to cluster greater over political topographies than over time; and indeed, preliminary tests demonstrated that predictive performance from resampling by year was virtually identical to that of not resampling by group at all, intimating that data leakage originating from temporal clustering is exceedingly marginal.

[^111]: Specifically, the dependencies `DoubleML` relies on for tuning (e.g., `mlr3`) do not offer the same sample-splitting procedure that cluster-robust DML uses. Though writing a custom resampling method to replicate it is theoretically possible, doing so would be time-intensive, and its accuracy would be hard to verify.

[^112]: Indeed, resampling by region and year jointly is technically possible: if country is simply ignored, leaving all $N$ rows intact, then it is certain that there will be multiple matches for each region and year. Doing so would complicate comparison with the country-year models, however, since the underlying resampling methods would be fundamentally different.

##### Final Fits {#sec-gen-dml-fits-final}

After optimizing all necessary or recommended hyperparameters, I run the final DML fits, generally as per the procedure and settings I outline in @sec-gen-dml-plr. Here, the last decision I make is to implement cluster-robust DML. Developed in Chiang et al. (2021), this DML variant rests on two pillars: grouped resampling during cross-fitting and cluster-robust standard errors. The first---similar to that discussed in @sec-gen-dml-fits-tune---ensures group-wise independence for each training- and test-set pair, thereby tempering data leakage. Unlike ordinary grouped resampling, however, wherein disjoint sets are defined by a single variable, cluster-robust DML permits multivariate sample splitting. Cross-fitting in the two-way (i.e., $K^{2}$) case proceeds as follows:[^113]

[^113]: My summary is adapted from Chiang et al. (2021, pp. 1050-1051) and the [`DoubleML` User Guide](https://docs.doubleml.org/stable/examples/R_double_ml_multiway_cluster.html).

1.  Assume that the data cluster along two dimensions, characterized by $N$ groups in the first and $M$ groups in the second. Accordingly, all observations are double-indexed, such that $\{\mathbf{S}_{ij} : i \in \{1, ..., N\}, j \in \{1, ..., M\}\}$.
2.  Randomly split $[N] := \{1, ..., N\}$ and $[M] := \{1, ..., M\}$ into $K$ user-specified subsets $\{I_{1}, ..., I_{K}\}$ and $\{J_{1}, ..., J_{K}\}$.
3.  Take the Cartesian product of these, giving $[K]^{2} := \{I_{1}, ..., I_{K}\} \times \{J_{1}, ..., J_{K}\}$ subsets.
4.  For each $(k, \ell) \in [K]^{2}$, estimate $\eta_{0}$ using all $(i, j)$ observations in $([N] \backslash I_{k}) \times ([M] \backslash J_{\ell})$. To wit:

$$
\hat{\eta}_{0, k\ell} = \hat{\eta}_{0}\left((\mathbf{S}_{ij})_{(i,j) \in ([N] \backslash I_{k}) \times ([M] \backslash J_{\ell})}\right)
$$

5.  Solve for causal estimate $\hat{\theta}_{0}$. Accommodating to the software-default algorithm given in @sec-gen-dml-plr:[^114]

[^114]: Here, $|I_{k}|$ and $|J_{\ell}|$ signify the cardinality of sets $I_{k}$ and $J_{\ell}$---that is, the number of groups belonging to each respective subset.

$$
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{K^{2}} \sum_{k = 1}^{K} \sum_{l = 1}^{K} \frac{1}{|I_{k}||J_{\ell}|} \sum_{(i, j) \in I_{k} \times J_{\ell}} \psi(\mathbf{S}_{ij}; \hat{\theta}_{0}, \hat{\eta}_{0,kl}) = 0
\end{align*}
$$

In this way, cross-fitting closely resembles the process detailed in @sec-gen-dml-plr, the key difference being the introduction of a modified grouped-resampling procedure guaranteeing mutual exclusivity between training and test sets along multiple dimensions at once.[^115]

[^115]: For those seeking further clarity, a practical, rudimentary demonstration of how $K^{2}$ sample splitting results in disjoint sets may be found in @sec-appx-f.

The second principal feature of cluster-robust DML---cluster-robust standard errors---is 

![DML Flowchart](/Users/briankey/Documents/Academics/Northwestern/dissertation/visualizations/memos/dml_flow_viz.png){#fig-dml-flow}

### Pooling {#sec-gen-pool}

![Pooling Flowchart](/Users/briankey/Documents/Academics/Northwestern/dissertation/visualizations/memos/pool_flow_viz.png){#fig-pool-flow}

## Conclusion {#sec-cncl}

Combined nuisance loss (RMSE of ml_l + ml_m) will be used to evaluate model performance (b/n ridge and tree-based models). For more on combined loss, see Bach et al. (2024b, p. 18).

## Bibliography

Ahrens, A., Chernozhukov, V., Hansen, C., Kozbur, D., Schaffer, M., & Wiemann, T. (2025). An Introduction to Double/Debiased Machine Learning (Version 1). arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/ARXIV.2504.08324

Bach, P., Kurz, M. S., Chernozhukov, V., Spindler, M., & Klaassen, S. (2024). DoubleML: An Object-Oriented Implementation of Double Machine Learning in R. Journal of Statistical Software, 108(3). https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v108.i03

Chernozhukov, V., Chetverikov, D., Demirer, M., Duflo, E., Hansen, C., Newey, W., & Robins, J. (2018). Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters. The Econometrics Journal, 21(1), C1–C68. https://doi.org/10.1111/ectj.12097

Chernozhukov, V., Hansen, C., Kallus, N., Spindler, M., & Syrgkanis, V. (2024). Applied Causal Inference Powered by ML and AI (No. arXiv:2403.02467). arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2403.02467

Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Wainwright, M. (2015). Statistical Learning with Sparsity: The Lasso and Generalizations. CRC Press, Taylor & Francis Group.

Hastie, T., Friedman, J., & Tisbshirani, R. (2017). The Elements of Statistical Learning: Data Mining, Inference, and Prediction, Second Edition (2nd ed.). Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-0-387-84858-7

Zou, H., & Hastie, T. (2005). Regularization and Variable Selection Via the Elastic Net. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B: Statistical Methodology, 67(2), 301–320. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9868.2005.00503.x

### Appendix D: Missingness Summaries {#sec-appx-d}

![Table Summarizing Missingness](/Users/briankey/Documents/Academics/Northwestern/dissertation/visualizations/memos/appx_d_viz.png){#fig-appx-d}

### Appendix E: PLR Score Function to OLS {#sec-appx-e}

Recall that the generic DML estimator solves the moment condition:

$$
\begin{align*}
\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}\psi(\mathbf{S};\hat{\theta}_{0},\hat{\eta}_{0}) = 0
\end{align*}
$$

Also recall the standard PLR score function, which may be further expanded:

$$
\begin{align*}
\psi(\mathbf{S};\theta,\eta) := [\mathbf{Y} - \ell(\mathbf{X}) - \theta(\mathbf{D} - m(\mathbf{X}))][\mathbf{D} - m(\mathbf{X})]\ \\
= -\theta(\mathbf{D} - m(\mathbf{X}))^{2} + (\mathbf{Y} - \ell(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - m(\mathbf{X}))
\end{align*}
$$

Solving the moment-condition estimator gives:[^116]

[^116]: The distribution of the mean operator $\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}\right)$ and the constant ($-\hat{\theta}$) follows from the linearity of expectation.

$$
\begin{align*}
0 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}[-\hat{\theta}_{0}(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))^{2} + (\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\ell}(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))] \\
\Longrightarrow 0 = -\hat{\theta}_{0}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))^{2} + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\ell}(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X})) \\
\Longrightarrow \hat{\theta}_{0}\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\ell}(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X})) \\
\Longrightarrow \hat{\theta}_{0} = \frac{\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\ell}(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))}{\sum_{i \in I} (\mathbf{D} - \hat{m}(\mathbf{X}))^{2}}
\end{align*}
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{align*}
\mathbf{\hat{Q}} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\ell}_{0}(\mathbf{X}) \\
\mathbf{\hat{V}} = \mathbf{D} - \hat{m}_{0}(\mathbf{X})
\end{align*}
$$

And where $\hat{\ell}_{0}$ and $\hat{m}_{0}$ are plug-in estimators of the conditional expectations:

$$
\begin{align*}
\ell_{0}(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y}|\mathbf{X}] \\
m_{0}(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D}|\mathbf{X}]
\end{align*}
$$

Now, consider a simple linear regression, wherein OLS predicts an outcome $\mathbf{Y}$ from a single treatment $\mathbf{D}$.[^117] The true coefficient on the explanator may be expressed as:[^118]

[^117]: To wit: $\mathbf{Y} = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}\mathbf{D} + \epsilon_{i}$

[^118]: That is, $\beta_{1} = \frac{\text{Cov}(\mathbf{D}, \mathbf{Y})}{\text{Var}(\mathbf{D})}$. In the bivariate case, $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D}] = \bar{\mathbf{D}}$ and $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y}] = \bar{\mathbf{Y}}$. The basic formula may be found in Rice (2007, p. 544), for example, albeit in scalar notation.

$$
\begin{align*}
\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y}])(\mathbf{D} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D}])}{\sum_{i \in I} (\mathbf{D} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D}])^{2}}
\end{align*}
$$

Or, in the multivariate case, with covariates $\mathbf{X}$, regressing residualized $\mathbf{Y}$ on residualized $\mathbf{D}$ implies (as per the FWL Theorem):

$$
\begin{align*}
\beta_{1} = \frac{\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{Y|X}])(\mathbf{D} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D|X}])}{\sum_{i \in I} (\mathbf{D} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{D|X}])^{2}} \\
= \frac{\sum_{i \in I}(\mathbf{Y} - \ell_{0}(\mathbf{X}))(\mathbf{D} - m_{0}(\mathbf{X}))}{\sum_{i \in I} (\mathbf{D} - m_{0}(\mathbf{X}))^{2}}
\end{align*}
$$

The solutions to $\hat{\theta}_{0}$ and the sample analogue $\hat{\beta}_{1}$ of $\beta_{1}$ are thus the selfsame, sufficing to illustrate how partialling out---that is, regressing $\mathbf{\hat{Q}}$ on $\mathbf{\hat{V}}$ to obtain $\hat{\theta}_{0}$ using OLS---implies the standard PLR score function.

### Appendix F: Example of Cluster-Robust DML Sample Splitting {#sec-appx-f}

To illustrate the sample-splitting procedure with a highly-simple scenario, consider a panel dataset comprised of countries $c \in [C] = \{1, ..., C\}$ and years $t \in [T] = \{1, ..., T\}$.[^119] In addition, let $C = 4$, $T = 4$, and $K = 2$. Now, suppose that the $C$ countries and $T$ years are randomly split into the following initial folds:

[^119]: Here, recalling the notation seen in @sec-gen-dml-fits-final, $C$ equates to $N$ and $T$ to $M$.

$$
\begin{align*}
I_{1} = \{c_{1}, c_{3}\} \qquad J_{1} = \{t_{2}, t_{3}\} \\
I_{2} = \{c_{2}, c_{4}\} \qquad J_{2} = \{t_{1}, t_{4}\}
\end{align*}
$$

Per the two-way cluster robust sample splitting operation, these form $K^{2} = 4$ folds:

$$
\begin{align*}
I_{1} \times J_{1} = \{(c_{1},t_{2}), (c_{1},t_{3}), (c_{3},t_{2}), (c_{3},t_{3})\} \\
I_{1} \times J_{2} = \{(c_{1},t_{1}), (c_{1},t_{4}), (c_{3},t_{1}), (c_{3},t_{4}\}) \\
I_{2} \times J_{1} = \{(c_{2},t_{2}), (c_{2},t_{3}), (c_{4},t_{2}), (c_{4},t_{3})\} \\
I_{2} \times J_{2} = \{(c_{2},t_{1}), (c_{2},t_{4}), (c_{4},t_{1}), (c_{4},t_{4})\}
\end{align*}
$$

Each is treated once as a test set, evaluating fits from corresponding training sets:

$$
\begin{align*}
I_{1}^{c} \times J_{1}^{c} = I_{2} \times J_{2} \\
I_{1}^{c} \times J_{2}^{c} = I_{2} \times J_{1} \\
I_{2}^{c} \times J_{1}^{c} = I_{1} \times J_{2} \\
I_{2}^{c} \times J_{2}^{c} = I_{1} \times J_{1}
\end{align*}
$$

How cluster-robust cross-fitting compares to the conventional approach summarized in @sec-gen-dml-plr is therefore clear. On the one hand, both guarantee observation-wise mutual exclusivity, guarding against the severest type of data leakage: selfsame observations appearing in corresponding training and test sets. On the other, cluster-robust cross-fitting goes a step further, enforcing group-wise disjointness as well. In doing so, training set shrinks.

### Appendix G: Model Runtimes {#sec-appx-g}
